claim: Type annotations using TypedDict are essential in LangGraph for defining state schemas with type safety, reducing runtime errors and improving code readability.
actionable: Replace dictionaries with TypedDict classes when defining LangGraph state structures to catch type mismatches at development time.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: LangGraph states must flow through nodes unidirectionally; when updating state attributes that were previously modified, use concatenation or appending rather than replacement to preserve conversation history.
actionable: When updating state in a node, use state.attribute += new_value instead of state.attribute = new_value to avoid wiping previous content in sequential graphs.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: Conditional edges in LangGraph route graph execution based on state conditions; use add_conditional_edges() with a routing function that returns edge names to implement multi-path workflows.
actionable: Define a routing function that inspects state and returns a string matching a path map key, then pass it to graph.add_conditional_edges(source_node, routing_func, path_map) to branch execution.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: LangGraph loops are implemented via conditional edges that route back to a prior node; set up a counter in state and check it in the routing function to limit iterations and prevent infinite loops.
actionable: Add a counter attribute to state, increment it in the node, then in the routing function return 'loop' if counter < max else 'exit' to control loop iterations.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: LLM integration in LangGraph requires docstrings on node functions; these docstrings are passed to the model to inform it what each function does when deciding which tools to call.
actionable: Always write clear, concise docstrings for every tool and node function—LLMs use these descriptions to understand what actions are available.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: ChatBot memory in LangGraph is implemented by appending both HumanMessage and AIMessage objects to a messages list in state; without persistence, the message history is lost when the program exits.
actionable: To persist conversation history across sessions, write the messages list to a text file or database at the end of each session, then load it when initializing state.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: In LangGraph chatbots, limiting conversation history length by removing early messages when the list exceeds a threshold (e.g., keep only last 5-10 exchanges) reduces token usage and API costs without losing recent context.
actionable: Implement a message pruning step: before invoking the LLM, if len(state.messages) > max_messages, remove the oldest message pairs to control token consumption.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: ReAct agents in LangGraph combine reasoning and acting: the agent decides which tool to call and when to stop tool calling; to implement this, use conditional edges that check if the last message contains tool_calls.
actionable: After invoking the model, inspect the last message's tool_calls attribute; if empty, route to end; if populated, route to tool_node and loop back to the agent.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: LangGraph tools are defined via the @tool decorator; tool docstrings must be specific (not placeholder text) because LLMs use these descriptions to decide when and how to call each tool.
actionable: Write tool docstrings that describe the tool's purpose, input parameters, and expected output in natural language so the LLM can understand when to use it.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: The add_messages reducer function in LangGraph automatically appends new messages to state rather than replacing them, preserving conversation history without manual append() calls.
actionable: Import add_messages from langraph.graph.message and use it as the reducer for sequence message state: messages: Annotated[Sequence[BaseMessage], add_messages].
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: LangGraph human-AI collaboration patterns: use one tool for content updates and another to save/finalize; route save operations directly to end (not back to agent) to conclude the workflow after user approval.
actionable: Design tool nodes with separate tools for iterative tasks (update) and terminal actions (save); use conditional edges to bypass the agent loop when the final tool is called.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: In RAG with LangGraph, chunk size (e.g., 1000 tokens) and overlap (e.g., 200 tokens) control retrieval granularity; overlap ensures important context at chunk boundaries is not lost when retrieving similar chunks.
actionable: When chunking documents for RAG, use chunk_size=1000 and chunk_overlap=200 as defaults; adjust overlap upward if retrieval misses boundary-spanning concepts.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: LangGraph RAG agents should instruct the LLM to cite specific document passages in its responses to minimize hallucination; include 'cite your sources from the document' in the system prompt.
actionable: Add to the system prompt: 'Always cite the specific parts of the document you use in your answers' to reduce hallucinations and verify information retrieval.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python

claim: LangGraph embedding models must be compatible with the LLM's training (e.g., OpenAI embeddings with GPT-4); mismatched models lead to poor retrieval because vector dimensions and semantic spaces differ.
actionable: When using a specific LLM (e.g., GPT-4), choose an embedding model from the same provider (OpenAI's text-embedding-3) to ensure vector space alignment.
source_title: LangGraph Complete Course for Beginners – Complex AI Agents with Python
